heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXXVII
card_summary: This chunk consists entirely of the scholarly endnotes (notes 49–90) to Campanini's introductory essay on Francesco Zorzi, covering the structure of the Problemata, censorship history of Zorzi's works, his kabbalistic combinatorics, reception by Bellarmino and Mersenne, biographical data, and his pilgrimage to the Holy Land. Key topics include: the thematic organization of all six tomes of the Problemata; Zorzi's question about the primacy of Hebrew as humanity's original language; the kabbalistic calculation of 1,124,002,590,817,719,680,000 possible letter-combinations representing created individuals; the Inquisitorial expurgation indices (Guanzelli/Brasichellensis 1607, Sandoval 1612, Sotomayor 1667) targeting both De harmonia mundi and the Problemata; Bellarmino's polemic against Zorzi's allegorical reading of the terrestrial paradise; Mersenne's annotations; a Latin epitaph for Zorzi found at San Francesco della Vigna in Venice; and bibliographic references to Pico della Mirandola's Conclusiones, Archangelo da Burgonovo's Apologia, and scholars including Secret, Vasoli, Wirszubski, and Campanini himself.
essay:
# Notes to the Introductory Essay (CXXVII): Endnotes 49–90

## Overview and Character of the Section

This chunk is the continuation of the apparatus of endnotes to Campanini's long introductory essay (*Saggio introduttivo*) to the Bompiani edition of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. It covers notes 49 through 90, spanning pages 127–131. Unlike the body of the essay, these notes function as both a bibliographic index and a repository of extended scholarly commentary, including direct quotations from Zorzi's texts, censorship documentation, biographical marginalia, and cross-references to the secondary literature. Several notes contain sufficiently substantial content to qualify as micro-essays in their own right.

## The Structure of the Problemata (Notes 49–54)

Notes 49 through 54 reproduce the detailed thematic tables of contents for each of the six tomes of Zorzi's *In Scripturam Sacram Problemata*. The tomes cover: (1) *De mundi fabrica* through *De Davide et aliis veris regibus*; (2) legal and cultic matters from *De quibusdam praecedentibus legem* to *De quibusdam consequentibus legem*; (3) prophetic oracles, Christology (including *De Christi conditione et adventu*, redemption, and eschatology); (4) the Gospel (*De Christo Deo*, *De Christo bellante et rege pacis*, *De Christo redemptore, sacerdote et hostia*, *De beata Virgine*); (5) wisdom literature alongside pagan sources — notably *De Mercurii Trismegisti dogmate*, *De placitis Academicorum*, and *De abstrusis Orphei, Pythagorae et Zorastris*; (6) natural philosophy and occult topics including *De coelis*, *De homine et eius viribus*, *De bonis et malis angelis*, *De operationibus quae fiunt a natura vel virtute aliqua*, and *De quibusdam occultis operationibus naturae et artis*.

The fifth tome is especially significant for Zorzi's syncretist programme: it places Solomon, Job, Hermes Trismegistus, the Academics (Platonists), Orpheus, Pythagoras, and Zoroaster in parallel, subordinating all pagan wisdom to Scripture.

## Hebrew as the Primordial Language (Note 60)

Note 60 quotes at length from *Problemata* 6,7,244 on the question of the original human language. Zorzi poses the problem: if a man had never learned to speak, what language would he use? He contests the common assumption that it would be Hebrew:

> Si numquam homo loqui didicisset, quo idiomate loqueretur? An forsitan hebraeo, ut vulgo dicitur? Sed cur magis hebraeo quam alio idiomate loqueretur? An forte quia hebraeum idioma primum omnium idiomatum fuit? Sed cum primus Hebraeorum fuit Abraham, qui ex Chaldaeis venit, nonne idioma chaldaicum praecessit? […] Nec aexistimo quod non addiscens aliquid idioma loqui debeat hebraeo vel chaldaeo sermone. Immo credo quod nullo modo loqueretur.

<cite>Problemata 6,7,244, quoted in note 60, p. 128</cite>

Zorzi's argument is empirically grounded: the congenitally deaf speak no language at all, which proves that language is entirely acquired. He denies that Hebrew possesses any natural, innate primacy — a position with implications for his kabbalistic hermeneutics, where the sacred status of Hebrew letters is theological and traditional rather than "natural."

## The Kabbalistic Combinatorics of Created Individuals (Note 61)

Note 61 is one of the most technically remarkable passages. It quotes *Problemata* 6,7,244 on the combinatorial enumeration of all possible created individuals, deploying a kabbalistic methodology in which Hebrew letters (*literae*) serve as the "material components" (*literae sunt materiales compositorum partes*) of all things:

> Ex omnibus enim literarum combinationibus secundum regulas secretorum theologorum infallibiles, absque punctorum aliqua immixtione hic numerus resultat, videlicet 1124002590817719680000. Si vero permixtiones cum punctis addantur, erit numerus nec expressibilis nec supputabilis a nobis, Deo tamen proculdubio cognitus.

<cite>Problemata 6,7,244, quoted in note 61, p. 128</cite>

The number 1,124,002,590,817,719,680,000 — derived from permutations of the Hebrew alphabet according to the "infallible rules of the secret theologians" (*regulas secretorum theologorum infallibiles*) — represents only those combinations without vowel-points (*puncta*). With the points, the total exceeds all human calculation, though it remains fully known to God. This passage situates Zorzi in the tradition of combinatorial Kabbalah flowing from Abulafia through Pico; the same figure appears in Archangelo da Burgonovo's *Apologia* (Bologna 1564, f. 267v; Basel 1600, p. 555) and in Blaise de Vigenère's *Traicté des chiffres* (Paris 1586, f. 42r), and was taken up by Mersenne in the *Harmonie universelle* (1636). Campanini also cites Umberto Eco (1993, p. 154).

## Censorship and Expurgation (Note 70)

Note 70 is the most extensive documentation of the Inquisitorial reception of Zorzi's work. Campanini itemizes the major expurgation indices:

- **Guanzelli (Brasichellensis)**, *Indicis librorum expurgandorum tomus primus* (Rome 1607), pp. 509–553: the most detailed official Catholic censorship of Zorzi, listing specific phrases to be deleted.
- **Sandoval**, *Index librorum prohibitorum et expurgatorum* (Madrid 1612), pp. 310–338; Geneva 1619, pp. 342–355: covers both *De harmonia mundi* and the *Problemata*.
- **Sotomayor**, *Index librorum prohibitorum et expurgandorum novissimus* (Madrid 1667), pp. 396–406.

The note also references the earlier mention of the Bessin/Alliot Paris 1622 edition of the *Problemata* (with its 16-page index of prohibited phrases appended), and cites Rotondò (1982), Fragnito (1997), Ricci (2008), Rebellato (2008), and especially Vasoli (2001) for secondary scholarship on the censorship.

## Bellarmino's Polemic Against Zorzi (Note 68)

Note 68 documents Cardinal Bellarmino's explicit refutation of a specific Zorzian position: that the *paradiso terrestre* (terrestrial paradise) was not to be understood literally. This is identified as a recurring claim in both *De harmonia mundi* and the *Problemata*. The reference is to Bellarmino's *Disputationes de controversiis christianae fidei* (1587), *De gratia primi hominis*, in *Opera omnia*, vol. IV (Naples 1858), pp. 36–37, with a cross-reference to Vasoli (1974, p. 136, n. 12).

## Mersenne's *Quaestiones in Genesim* (Note 69)

Note 69 cites Mersenne's *Quaestiones celeberrimae in Genesim* (Paris 1623), which includes *Observationes et emendationes ad Francisci Georgii Veneti Problemata* — a detailed critical engagement with Zorzi's exegesis. Campanini refers to Vasoli (1981), pp. 281–295 for analysis.

## The Epitaph at San Francesco della Vigna (Note 81)

Note 81 reproduces a previously unedited Latin epitaph for Zorzi, found by Campanini himself in a copy of the *Problemata* at the library of the convent of San Francesco della Vigna in Venice:

> Post mundi numeros, et sacra aenigmata legis
> Ad quaesita rogat carmine docta Patres.
> Dura haec saxa tegunt quod fatis debuit, alta
> Mens habet, et Summi vivit in arce Iovis.

<cite>Epitaphium Francisci Georgi Veneti, copied from convent library ms., quoted in note 81, p. 130</cite>

This version omits Zorzi's age at death. Campanini uses this to argue against Degli Agostini's figure of 80 years: if Zorzi was born in 1466 and died in 1540, he was 73, not 80. He also notes that the 1780 source (Trieste de' Pellegrini) gives the erroneous death year MDXXX (1530).

## Zorzi's Pilgrimage to the Holy Land (Notes 88–90)

Notes 88–90 address Zorzi's documented pilgrimage to the Holy Land. Campanini explicitly challenges Vasoli (1974 and 2008) for doubting that the pilgrimage actually took place, citing his own 2009 response (Campanini 2009c). The secondary literature cited includes Secret (1968a; 1974; 1975) and the bibliography compiled by Khan, Maillard, and Brach (2001).

## Biographical and Textual Notes

Note 76 reproduces a laudatory hexameter poem (*carmen*) prefixed to the *Problemata*, praising Zorzi for illuminating what the prophets and Moses had left in obscuring darkness:

> Dilucent nunc luce tua, lux alma Georgi.

<cite>Carmen prefixed to the Problemata, quoted in note 76, p. 129</cite>

Notes 72–75 situate Zorzi's kabbalistic work in relation to Pico della Mirandola's *Conclusiones* and Archangelo da Burgonovo's *Apologia*, and to manuscript traditions (Fondo Piancastelli, cod. VII/57, noted by Kristeller 1965). Note 80 clarifies that cross-references to the *Problemata* in a Venetian manuscript miscellany (cc. 113r–135v) rule out the identification of its copyist with Cardinal Cristoforo Numai, who died in 1528.

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

Taken together, these notes illuminate three dimensions of Zorzi's project: (1) the encyclopaedic scope of the *Problemata* as a parallel to *De harmonia mundi*, embracing pagan wisdom (Hermetic, Platonic, Orphic, Pythagorean, Zoroastrian) within a biblical framework; (2) the central role of Hebrew combinatorics in Zorzi's cosmology, where the kabbalistic permutation of letters provides a mathematical foundation for the doctrine of universal harmony; (3) the post-Tridentine fate of this synthesis, suppressed by systematic expurgation even as it continued to fascinate Mersenne, de Vigenère, and the broader European tradition of Christian Kabbalah.
